classification.label: Discovery + Demo (Working Session)
signals: Sam shares his own live Plotline account and walks through features in real time
Interleaved probing of Dana's current pain ('nobody touches it except one analyst') alongside live product walkthrough
Early first working session that both qualifies (event volume, current tool, bottleneck) and demonstrates simultaneously
Founder-led motion with no separation between discovery and demo phases

outcome.intendedOutcome: Uncover Crater Labs' product analytics pain, demonstrate Plotline against that pain, identify the buying process and economic buyer, differentiate from Cohortly, and book a concrete next step.
desiredExitCriteria: At least one business pain tied to a metric or dollar impact
Demo anchored to pains surfaced in the same call, not a generic tour
Economic buyer and decision process identified
Differentiation vs incumbent or alternative made explicit
Mutual next step scheduled on the call
missedCriteria: At least one business pain tied to a metric or dollar impact
Demo anchored to pains surfaced in the same call, not a generic tour
Economic buyer and decision process identified
Differentiation vs incumbent or alternative made explicit
Mutual next step scheduled on the call
outcome.actualOutcome: Sam ran a product-led, feature-tour demo without first discovering Dana's specific pains or metrics. Dana made a passing remark about Cohortly underutilization but it was never explored. Pricing was deflected, no decision process was uncovered, differentiation from Cohortly was never articulated, and the call ended with a vague promise to send a trial login rather than a scheduled follow-up.
outcome.advanceDetail: No specific, time-bound next step was committed. Sam said 'I'll send you a login, play with it whenever' and Dana responded noncommittally with 'Yeah, maybe.' No date, meeting, or defined action was agreed upon.
gaps: Never conducted discovery to quantify pain — no metrics, no dollar impact, no understanding of how Cohortly underutilization hurts Crater Labs
Demo was a generic feature tour driven by Sam's enthusiasm, not anchored to Dana's expressed problems
Economic buyer never identified — Dana's title is VP Product but her authority to purchase was never confirmed
Decision process, stakeholders, and timeline were never explored
Cohortly was mentioned by Dana but Sam never explicitly differentiated Plotline against it beyond an implied ease-of-use suggestion
No mutual next step with a date was secured; pricing was avoided entirely

evidence.speaker: Sam
evidence.quote: I'll just share my screen and show you what we've been building — this is literally my own Plotline account

evidence.speaker: Dana
evidence.quote: We're on Cohortly right now and honestly nobody touches it except one analyst.

evidence.speaker: Sam
evidence.quote: We're super flexible, we'll figure something out that works, I'm not worried about it.

evidence.speaker: Dana
evidence.quote: We're kind of heads-down on a launch for the next few weeks. But this is neat.

evidence.speaker: Sam
evidence.quote: I'll send you a login, play with it whenever. We move fast, so ping me anytime.

coaching.headline: High-energy demo that built rapport but skipped discovery, left all MEDDPICC elements blank, and closed with a self-serve trial instead of a committed next step — the deal is functionally stuck.

sellingStyle.note: Sam's live-account demo and personal backstory created authentic credibility fast, but founder-led energy ran straight into product enthusiasm at the expense of structured discovery — the same authenticity that opened Dana up was never leveraged to slow down and mine her pain, and the call ended with no advance.

strengths: Immediate credibility: using his own live Plotline account and sharing the YC/PM backstory created genuine trust and lowered Dana's guard quickly.
Pain resonance moment: Sam's 'one person becomes the bottleneck' framing visibly connected with Dana's Cohortly situation and surfaced a real, buyer-stated pain.
Product demonstration was crisp and concrete — the natural-language query live in the call was the strongest value signal of the entire conversation.
gaps: Zero discovery before or during the demo: Sam never asked about Dana's goals, current workflow pain, team size, or what success would look like — all MEDDPICC dimensions are empty.
No advance secured: the call ended with an open-ended trial login and no date, owner, or defined next step, meaning the deal has no momentum.

Pain surfaced but immediately abandoned: when Dana revealed the Cohortly bottleneck, Sam said 'Right??' and moved on to the next feature rather than probing urgency, consequences, or quantified impact.
Pricing deflected entirely: Sam's 'we'll figure something out' response to Dana's pricing question missed a qualification and anchoring opportunity.
Cost of inaction never raised: Sam accepted Dana's 'heads-down on a launch' objection without reframing it as a reason to move faster, leaving no urgency in the deal.

dimensions.label: Metrics
dimensions.rationale: No success metrics or quantified business impact were established; Dana's pain was touched on but never tied to numbers the buyer owns.
dimensions.betterMove: After Dana says 'nobody touches it except one analyst,' ask: 'What does that bottleneck actually cost you — do you know how many product decisions get delayed or made without data each sprint?' Then anchor a number.

dimensions.label: Economic Buyer
dimensions.rationale: No mention of budget authority, who signs contracts, or who else is involved in a purchase decision of this kind.
dimensions.betterMove: Ask Dana directly: 'For a tool that would sit in the product team's stack, who would be involved in the final call — is that you, or do you need your CTO or finance involved above a certain spend threshold?'

dimensions.label: Decision Criteria
dimensions.rationale: Dana never stated what Crater Labs would need to see to choose a new analytics tool; Sam did not ask.
dimensions.betterMove: Ask: 'If you were to move off Cohortly, what would the new tool have to prove — technically and in terms of adoption — for you to feel confident recommending it internally?'

dimensions.label: Decision Process
dimensions.rationale: No steps, owners, or timeline toward a decision were discussed; the call ended with no mapped path forward.
dimensions.betterMove: Before closing, ask: 'You mentioned a launch in the next few weeks — what does your calendar look like after that, and is that a realistic window to evaluate something like this with the right people in the room?'

dimensions.label: Paper Process
dimensions.rationale: Procurement, legal, or security requirements were never mentioned.
dimensions.betterMove: At minimum, plant the flag early: 'Companies your size often have a security review or vendor onboarding process — is that something we should loop in early so it doesn't slow us down later?'

dimensions.label: Identify Pain
dimensions.rationale: A surface-level pain (low adoption of Cohortly, single analyst bottleneck) surfaced from Dana, but Sam moved on without probing urgency, consequences, or ownership.

evidence.speaker: Dana
evidence.quote: We're on Cohortly right now and honestly nobody touches it except one analyst.

dimensions.betterMove: When Dana reveals the bottleneck, stop the demo and dig in: 'How often does that slow down a product decision? What happens when a PM needs an answer and the analyst isn't available — do they ship without data or just wait?'

dimensions.label: Champion
dimensions.rationale: Dana's internal influence, willingness to advocate, or ability to sell internally was never explored; Sam treated Dana as an end user, not a potential champion.
dimensions.betterMove: Test champion potential by asking: 'If this solved the bottleneck you described, would you be in a position to bring this to the rest of the product team, or would that need to come from someone above you?'

dimensions.label: Competition
dimensions.rationale: Cohortly was named as the current tool but Sam did not probe dissatisfaction depth, switching costs, or differentiate Plotline against it in a meaningful way.

evidence.speaker: Dana
evidence.quote: We're on Cohortly right now and honestly nobody touches it except one analyst.

dimensions.betterMove: Ask: 'What's kept you on Cohortly despite the low adoption — is it contract lock-in, data migration concerns, or something else?' Then tailor the differentiation to those specific switching objections.

dimensions.label: Required Capabilities
dimensions.rationale: Sam made generic claims about ease of use and speed but did not explicitly connect Plotline's capabilities to Dana's stated bottleneck in a structured way.

evidence.speaker: Sam
evidence.quote: Everyone buys these heavy tools and then one person becomes the bottleneck.

dimensions.betterMove: After Dana confirms the single-analyst problem, pivot: 'Let me show you specifically how a PM at Crater could answer that same question today without touching the analyst — watch what happens when I type it in plain English.' Then map that capability directly to their pain.

dimensions.label: Positive Business Outcomes
dimensions.rationale: No business outcome was framed or quantified; Sam stayed in feature-demonstration mode throughout.
dimensions.betterMove: Frame the value before the demo ends: 'If every PM on your team could get answers in ten seconds instead of waiting for the analyst, how many more data-informed decisions could you ship in a quarter? That's the number we want to make real for you.'

dimensions.label: Differentiation (Why Us)
dimensions.rationale: Sam implied speed and simplicity versus 'heavy tools' but never named Cohortly directly in a differentiated comparison or gave Dana language she could repeat.

evidence.speaker: Sam
evidence.quote: Everyone buys these heavy tools and then one person becomes the bottleneck.

evidence.speaker: Sam
evidence.quote: We built Plotline to answer that in ten seconds. Watch — I'll ask it: 'retention of users who hit the new onboarding flow.' Boom, there's the cohort, no SQL.

dimensions.betterMove: Make the comparison explicit and give Dana a repeatable line: 'The difference from Cohortly is that Cohortly is built for analysts — Plotline is built for PMs. You shouldn't need a translator to read your own product data.' Then confirm she can articulate that back.

dimensions.label: Why Now (Cost of Inaction)
dimensions.rationale: No cost of inaction, compelling event, or urgency was established; Sam actually accepted Dana's 'heads-down on a launch' as a reason to defer without challenging it.
dimensions.betterMove: When Dana mentions the launch, reframe: 'That launch is actually the perfect forcing function — what if we ran a POV during it so you can see exactly which users engage with the new feature in real time, without waiting on the analyst? That's when the value is most obvious.'

dimensions.label: Proof
dimensions.rationale: Sam referenced having 'customers way bigger than that' on event volume but offered no specific customer name, metric, or story to substantiate the claim.

evidence.speaker: Sam
evidence.quote: Oh, totally fine, we've got customers way bigger than that.

dimensions.betterMove: Replace the vague claim with a tailored proof point: 'We have a B2B SaaS company doing 400M events a month — they ran a POV in two weeks and their analyst time on ad-hoc queries dropped by 60%. Happy to make an intro if that's useful.'

dimensions.label: Control and Advance
dimensions.rationale: The call ended with 'I'll send you a login, play with it whenever' — a self-serve trial with no date, no owner, no defined next step, and no advance.

evidence.speaker: Sam
evidence.quote: I'll send you a login, play with it whenever. We move fast, so ping me anytime.

dimensions.betterMove: Before hanging up, say: 'Rather than an open-ended trial, let me propose something more useful — a 30-minute working session next week where I load your actual events and we answer three questions your team has been waiting on. Does Thursday or Friday work?' Then book it on the call.

topActions: Book a structured working session before sending the trial login: email Dana today and propose a specific date — 'Rather than an open trial, let me suggest a 45-minute session where we connect your actual event data and answer three real questions your team has. Can we do Thursday May 7th at 2pm ET?' This converts a soft lead into a concrete advance.

Run a pain excavation before every demo: prepare five discovery questions for Dana's role (e.g., 'How many PMs on your team make decisions without data today?' 'What's the analyst queue time for an ad-hoc query?') and ask at least three before touching the screen share — the demo only lands if the pain is already quantified.

Identify the economic buyer and map the decision process on the working session call: open with 'Dana, assuming this works for you, walk me through how a tool like this gets approved at Crater — who else needs to be involved and what does that process look like?' then ask to include that person in the next meeting.
